cards.title: Heliotherapy — Therapeutic Use of Sunlight
cards._legacyHtml:
Heliotherapy Definition
Heliotherapy is the therapeutic application of natural or artificial sunlight for healing. It is one of the oldest and most fundamental naturopathic modalities — Hippocrates, Paracelsus, and Finsen all used light therapeutically. The therapeutic effects are mediated through UV radiation (UVA, UVB), visible light, and infrared radiation acting on skin, circadian biology, and photobiochemical pathways.
ComponentWavelengthPrimary ActionKey Therapeutic Effect
UVB280–315 nmPhotolysis of 7-dehydrocholesterol → pre-vitamin D3Vitamin D synthesis, psoriasis treatment, immune modulation
UVA315–400 nmMelanin oxidation, nitric oxide release from skinTanning, vasodilation, blood pressure reduction
Visible Light400–700 nmPhotoreceptor activation (ipRGC → SCN)Circadian entrainment, melatonin suppression, mood elevation
Infrared (Near-IR)700–1400 nmCytochrome c oxidase activation (photobiomodulation)Tissue healing, ATP synthesis, pain reduction, mitochondrial function
Infrared (Mid/Far-IR)1400nm–1mmThermal tissue heatingVasodilation, muscle relaxation (sauna), detoxification

cards.title: Practical Heliotherapy Protocols
cards._legacyHtml:
1
Morning Sunlight Protocol (Circadian) 10–30 minutes of outdoor light within 30–60 minutes of waking. Look towards sun (NOT directly at it) — activates non-image-forming ipRGC receptors in retina that signal SCN (suprachiasmatic nucleus). Blue-sky light (even cloudy) contains sufficient 480nm signal. This is the strongest circadian zeitgeber — sets cortisol awakening response, improves sleep onset at night, stabilises melatonin rhythm. Evidence: 4× lower depression risk, 50% faster sleep onset.
2
Vitamin D Sunlight Protocol Expose forearms, legs, and back during UVB peak hours (10am–2pm). Duration: Fitzpatrick Type I–II (fair) = 5–10 min; Type III–IV = 15–25 min; Type V–VI (dark) = 25–40 min. Shorter exposure more frequent is better than long infrequent. Glass blocks 97% of UVB — must be outdoors. SPF 15 reduces Vit D synthesis by 98% — time unprotected exposure before applying sunscreen. Sunscreen after UV exposure has already occurred.
3
Full-Body Sunbathing (Airing / Air Bath) Gradual exposure starting 5–10 min, increasing to 30–60 min over weeks. Begin early morning (7–9am) to avoid peak UV. Patient exposed on all sides, turning every 10 minutes. Ensure head covered, eyes protected. Monitor for erythema — stop at first sign of redness. Suitable for: general debility, anaemia, psoriasis (careful — photosensitivity check), chronic respiratory conditions, depression, musculoskeletal pain.
4
Partial Heliotherapy Target specific body regions with sunlight. Back exposure: prone position, 15–20 min — beneficial for vertebral pain, spondylitis. Abdomen: supine, 10–20 min — for chronic constipation, digestive disorders (controversial, skin warming increases GI motility). Limbs: for dermatological conditions, peripheral circulatory disorders. Always use graduated exposure — start shorter.
ConditionHeliotherapy ProtocolEvidenceNotes
PsoriasisNB-UVB phototherapy (311nm) — artificial or natural UVB★★★ Grade AGold standard for moderate-severe psoriasis. 3x/week. Remission in 70-80%. Risk: skin ageing, long-term cancer risk (monitor)
Vitamin D DeficiencyDaily 15–30 min midday UVB exposure + oral supplementation★★★ Grade AMonitor 25(OH)D — aim 75–150 nmol/L. Oral 2000–4000 IU/day if sunlight insufficient
Seasonal Affective Disorder (SAD)10,000 lux bright light therapy box, 20–30 min, morning★★★ Grade AComparable to antidepressants. Begin September, use through winter. Artificial light preferred for precision
Neonatal JaundiceBlue light phototherapy (460–490nm) in NICU★★★ Grade AConverts bilirubin to water-soluble isomers. Natural sunlight through glass inadequate — use phototherapy unit
Chronic LBP / SpondylitisBack heliotherapy 20–30 min daily (as tolerated)★ Grade CVitamin D + local warmth + mood elevation contribute. Combine with hydrotherapy and yoga
Depression / Mood DisordersMorning sunlight 20–30 min + bright light therapy★★★ Grade ACircadian entrainment, serotonin upregulation. Strongest non-pharmacological intervention after exercise

cards.title: Aerotherapy — Therapeutic Use of Air
cards._legacyHtml:
Aerotherapy Definition
Aerotherapy uses the therapeutic properties of air — composition, temperature, humidity, movement, and altitude — for health promotion and disease management. Sea air (negative ions, sea spray), mountain air (altitude, purity), and forest air (phytoncides, terpenes) each have distinct physiological effects recognised in naturopathic practice.
Air TypeActive ComponentsPhysiological EffectTherapeutic Application
Sea AirNegative ions, NaCl aerosol, iodine, ozone tracesSerotonin upregulation, mucociliary clearance, bronchodilationAsthma, COPD, depression, rhinitis, skin conditions
Forest Air (Shinrin-Yoku)Phytoncides (α-pinene, β-pinene, limonene from trees), terpenesNK cell activation (↑ up to 50%), cortisol ↓, BP ↓, HRV ↑Hypertension, immune support, stress, anxiety, fatigue
Mountain AirReduced O₂ partial pressure, UV intensity ↑, low pollutionEPO stimulation → RBC production, pulmonary adaptationAnaemia, altitude training, chronic fatigue
Air Baths (Airing)Direct skin contact with ambient air, thermoreceptionHardening response — improved autonomic thermoregulation, immune activationGeneral health promotion, constitution strengthening, respiratory conditions
Negative Ion TherapyNegative ions (O₂⁻) from moving water, forest, after rainSerotonin normalisation, reduced airborne pathogens, improved alertnessDepression, respiratory allergies, sick building syndrome

cards.title: Aerotherapy Protocols — Practical Application
cards._legacyHtml:
1
Air Bath Protocol (Luftbad) Traditional European naturopathic practice: expose skin to open air progressively. Begin with 10 min exposure at comfortable temperature (warm weather). Gradually extend to 30–60 min. Progress to cooler temperatures over weeks (hardening). Optimal: morning air, light exercise during exposure. Combine with sunlight for synergistic effect. Beneficial for: general constitution, skin conditions, respiratory health, psychological wellbeing.
2
Forest Bathing (Shinrin-Yoku) Protocol 2-hour slow walk in forest — no exercise intensity target. 5 senses engagement: touch tree bark, listen to bird sounds, smell forest floor. Best: coniferous forests (highest phytoncide concentration). Frequency: 2x/month minimum for NK cell benefits (effect lasts up to 30 days). Evidence: NK cell activity ↑ 50% after 3-day forest trip. City park: some benefit but lower phytoncide concentration.
3
Sea Air Therapy Recommend coastal walks or residence for: chronic respiratory conditions (asthma, COPD, chronic sinusitis), eczema/psoriasis, depression, immune deficiency. Duration: minimum 30 min daily if resident; therapeutic stays of 2–4 weeks for chronic conditions. Salt aerosol (halotherapy) available via salt caves if coastal access unavailable. Evidence: significant improvement in airway inflammation markers, FEV₁ in COPD.
4
Deep Breathing Exercises (Aerotherapy + Pranayama) Full lung ventilation in clean outdoor air: diaphragmatic breathing 5-7 min, followed by retention (kumbhaka), then slow exhalation. Best in garden, forest, or near water. Enhances lymphatic drainage, increases alveolar oxygen delivery, activates parasympathetic NS, reduces CO₂ accumulation in poorly ventilated lungs. Use Buteyko breathing for asthma (controlled breath reduction technique).
🟡 Heliotherapy + Aerotherapy — Combined Protocol
The most potent combination in naturopathic outpatient practice: morning walk in nature (forest or garden), 20–30 minutes, before 10am. Patient receives simultaneously: morning light (circadian entrainment), phytoncides (NK cell activation), fresh air with negative ions, moderate exercise (endorphin release), grounding (barefoot contact if possible — earthing reduces cortisol, reduces inflammatory markers). Evidence base for each component is individually strong — combined effect is synergistic and represents the core of naturopathic lifestyle prescription.

cards.title: Contraindications & Precautions — Helio & Aerotherapy
cards._legacyHtml:
TherapyAbsolute ContraindicationsRelative / Precautions
HeliotherapyPhotosensitising medications (tetracyclines, fluoroquinolones, thiazides, amiodarone, NSAIDs — strong photosensitisers). Xeroderma pigmentosum. Active SLE (UV triggers flares). Photodermatoses (polymorphous light eruption).Fair skin (Fitzpatrick I–II) — limit duration; always monitor for erythema. Personal or family history of melanoma — use judiciously with dermatology input. Active vitiligo — some respond; others may worsen. Recent skin surgery at exposure site.
Aerotherapy (General)Severe COPD with hypoxic drive (high-altitude aerotherapy — O₂ reduction dangerous). Active pulmonary tuberculosis — no communal air bath settings.Cold air: caution in Raynaud's, cold-induced asthma (exercise-induced bronchoconstriction — use bronchodilator pre-activity). High pollen environments: contraindicate outdoor aerotherapy during peak pollen season in allergic rhinitis / allergic asthma.
Bright Light TherapyBipolar disorder (may trigger mania — use only with psychiatrist supervision in bipolar). Retinal disease (macular degeneration, diabetic retinopathy). Photosensitising medications.Migraine (bright light may trigger attack — use lower intensity, 2500 lux, longer duration). ADHD medications (stimulants already affect circadian — monitor carefully). Use 10,000 lux box at 40–60cm — never look directly at box.
Documentation Note
Always record: baseline skin type (Fitzpatrick scale), medications (for photosensitivity), exposure duration, UV index on day of treatment, and patient response. For phototherapy: record body surface area treated, MED (minimal erythemal dose) testing results, cumulative UV dose.
